title: Monoset
desc: A minimal, monotone, unopinionated design system.
body: Monoset design system. Tokens and React components. One neutral ramp. One typeface. Radix-backed components. Monotone. Minimal. Unopinionated.

title: Introduction
desc: A neutral token system and React component library that leaves product decisions to you.
body: Monoset is a neutral token system and React component library. It gives you the common UI pieces while leaving the product decisions to you.

title: Installation
desc: Install Monoset via the CSS tokens or the React component kit.
body: Three ways in, depending on how deep you're going. The starter CLI is the fastest for a new project, the React kit is the usual pick, and the tokens-only package is there if you're not using React. Creates a Vite + React 19 project with the Monoset package and CSS imports in place. Install the component package and tokens in an existing React app. Motion helpers are available from the React motion subpath. @monoset/tokens/css includes variables, base element styles, and typography. If the application owns its reset and typography, import only the variables. The React stylesheet comes after either option. If you're building components yourself, or your stack isn't React, pull in just the token file and style against the CSS variables. Inter for UI, JetBrains Mono for code. Both open source, both self-hosted. The easiest path is @fontsource : one CSS import per weight, files get bundled by Vite, zero third-party requests. Prefer Google Fonts instead? Drop a <link> into your HTML head and skip the npm packages. The tokens work either way. React v1 removes the old form-state and stagger helpers. It also moves motion helpers out of the package root. The old TableHeader sort prop becomes sortable with sortDirection . Replace Form and useMonosetForm with React state or your existing form library. Replace StaggerList with an application-owned motion composition if you still need it. Starter CLI (new project) React kit (existing project) CSS tokens only (any framework) Fonts React v1 migration

title: Installation · Choose the CSS entry points
Basic usage
desc: An end-to-end example with Monoset tokens and primitives.
body: A quick end-to-end example: a sign-in card built with Monoset tokens and primitives. Sign in Use your workspace email. Continue `} Dark theme Add the monoset-dark class to any element and the semantic tokens flip for everything inside it. No ThemeProvider, no context. Put it on <html> for a whole-app switch, or on a single panel for an inverted card. Full example Dark theme

title: Colors
desc: The 12-step neutral ramp and the semantic tokens built on top of it.
body: One 12-step neutral ramp. Backgrounds, text, borders, and shadows all pull from it. No hues and no gradients. The constraint is the whole point. Reach for the semantic tokens ( --bg , --fg1 , --border ) inside components. Raw ramp values ( --mono-500 ) are for defining new semantic tokens, not for sprinkling into UI. Otherwise the dark-mode flip misses you. Neutral ramp Semantic tokens

title: Typography
desc: Inter for UI, JetBrains Mono for code, a modular scale from 11px to 64px.
body: Inter for UI and body, JetBrains Mono for code. A scale from 11px to 64px (hit ratios, not arbitrary sizes) with two ways to create contrast: tone and size. Weight does some of the work too, but tone carries it. Type scale Families

title: Spacing & radii
desc: A 4px base grid with named spacing and radius tokens.
body: A 4px base grid with named tokens. Components snap to these values; resist the urge to sneak in a stray 7px padding because it "looks better". It usually doesn't, and the system falls apart one off-grid value at a time. Everything that isn't a pill uses --radius-md (6px). Keeping one radius across inputs, buttons, cards and menus makes surfaces feel like they came from the same kit. --radius-full is reserved for avatars and status chips. Spacing scale Radii

title: Motion
desc: CSS motion tokens and the optional React motion entry.
body: Three durations and one easing curve. Fast and quiet by design: no springs, no bounces, nothing that hangs around for 900ms. Motion should confirm what just happened, then get out of the way. When CSS transitions hit their limit, like FLIP layout animations or anything that needs to interrupt itself mid-play, reach for Framer Motion. Same rules as the CSS tokens: short durations, one curve, no springs. Import React motion helpers from the motion subpath. They are kept out of the package root so static component imports do not pull them in. Start here. whileHover and whileTap cover 80% of what you'll ever need. Pick one property (a 2px lift or a shade), keep it under 180ms, and don't stack them. Hover me Shade on hover Enter on mount A small fade and 8px rise on mount. Use it for first-paint reveals, not for every re-render. The animation loses meaning if it plays every time state changes. Mount / unmount Without AnimatePresence , unmounts just vanish. Wrap conditional children and they get an exit. Monoset's exit is a quick opacity drop, no slide, no scale; sliding panels off-screen is noisier than they deserve. setOpen(o => !o)} style= }> panel `} Layout transitions Share a layoutId across mounted siblings and Framer does the FLIP math. The tab indicator below slides between items without you writing a single pixel offset. style= }> ))} `} Rules , , ) for UI. It introduces bounce. Reserve springs for physical drag." }, , ].map((r,i) => ( ))} Reduced motion MonosetProvider configures Framer Motion with reducedMotion="user" , and Monoset components use targeted CSS media queries. Add reduced-motion behavior to animation owned by your application. Easing Durations Install Monoset motion presets Hover & press Enter on mount Mount / unmount Layout transitions Rules Reduced motion

title: Button
desc: Four variants, three sizes, four states. A React Button component.
body: Four variants across three sizes. Keep labels short (two or three words) and write them in sentence case. All-caps looks shouty in a monotone system. Variants Sizes States

title: Input
desc: Native input and textarea props with Field label and error wiring.
body: Text fields, textareas, selects. Wrap them in a Field to connect the label, description, and error state to the control. } /> States Textarea Multi-line input, same styling and the same Field pattern. It accepts every native textarea attribute; rows defaults to 4. Field States Textarea

title: Select
desc: A dropdown for picking one option from a list. Wraps Radix UI Select.
body: A list for choosing one option. It works with keyboard and screen reader input. The selected item is marked, and long lists stay inside the viewport. Admin Member Viewer Billing Admin Member Viewer Billing `} With placeholder The placeholder prop on SelectTrigger shows when no value is selected. Field works with native React state and any form library. Use Field.Control to pass its accessibility props to the select trigger. SelectContent stays inside the available viewport. Scroll controls appear when the options do not fit, and the selected item keeps a visible check mark. Default With placeholder In a form Long lists

title: Checkbox & Switch
desc: Two similar controls with different semantics.
body: Two similar-looking controls with different semantics. Use a checkbox when the choice is part of a form that gets submitted. Use a switch when flipping it takes effect right away, like email notifications or dark mode. Checkbox Switch

title: Radio group
desc: Pick one from a few. Use Select for longer lists.
body: Pick one from a few. If the list is longer than three or four options, use Select or Combobox instead. from "@monoset/react"; `} API void", default:"—", desc:"Called when selection changes." }, , ]}/> Each Radio takes a value and a label . It can be disabled individually with disabled . Basic radio group API

title: Combobox
desc: A searchable select for picking one option from a long list.
body: An editable input with a filtered list of options. Use it when a Select would be slow to scan. Arrow keys move through results, Enter selects, and Escape closes the list. Add a description when a short label needs more context. The default filter matches against label, description, and an optional keywords array. Use keywords for terms you want to find but not show. If two rows share a label, give each one a unique textValue . This is the text shown in the input after selection and the text announced by assistive technology. Pass a filter function for non-default matching, like prefix-only or fuzzy. Use Field.Control to connect the label, help text, and error message to the editable input. Add name when the selected value should be submitted with a form. Basic usage With descriptions Search keywords Duplicate labels Custom filter In a form API

title: Multi-select Combobox
desc: Searchable Combobox that selects multiple values.
body: An editable multi-select that keeps each selected value as a removable tag. It stays searchable as the list grows, and the list marks every selected option. Paste comma-separated or line-separated values to add several options at once. Exact values, labels, and textValue strings are accepted. Text that does not match stays in the input so it is not lost. Pass name to submit one value for each selected option. The component also supports an external form through form . Basic Paste several values Form values API

title: DatePicker
desc: Calendar in a popover for picking a single date.
body: An editable date field with a calendar popover. It uses CalendarDate values, so dates stay independent of time zones. Pass min and max to bound selection. Basic Min and max API

title: Calendar
desc: Standalone month grid. Inline, always visible. DatePicker is built on it.
body: A standalone month grid for CalendarDate values. Locale settings control its labels and default week start. The locale sets the default. Pass firstDayOfWeek="sun" to override it. Basic Week start API

title: Number input
desc: Text input with +/- stepper buttons for numeric fields.
body: A locale-aware number field with stepper buttons. It keeps unfinished input such as - or 1, intact until the user commits it. Pass locale to control decimal parsing. formatOptions accepts standard Intl.NumberFormat options. Basic Locale and formatting API

title: Pin input
desc: Multi-cell input for OTP and short codes. Auto-advance, paste support.
body: A fixed row of one-character fields for verification codes. Editing a middle cell leaves the later cells in place. Paste and one-time-code autofill start at the active cell. />`} Editing and paste Arrow keys move between cells. Backspace on an empty cell moves to the previous one. Pasted text is filtered through pattern and fills the remaining cells from the current position. Pass name to submit the current code as one value. required keeps the form invalid until every cell is filled. External forms and native reset are supported. Basic Editing and paste In a form API

title: Password input
body: An Input with a Show / Hide toggle on the right. Tabs to the toggle so keyboard users can flip visibility too. API Other props forward to Input . Basic API

title: File upload
desc: Drop zone with click-to-pick fallback and accessible keyboard handling.
body: A native file picker presented as a dropzone. The picker handles pointer and keyboard use; dropped files follow the same selection rules. Selected files stay in a removable list. Selection behavior Each pick or drop replaces the current batch. In single-file mode only the first accepted file is kept. The native picker is cleared after every change, so choosing the same file again still calls onFilesChange . Picker and drop input use the same accept rules: extensions, exact MIME types, and wildcards such as image/* . onFilesRejected receives files that do not match. A fully rejected batch leaves the current selection alone. Pass name to append each selected file to form data under that key. form associates the picker with an external form. Required validity and disabled omission follow native form behavior. Basic Selection behavior Rejected files In a form API

title: Slider
desc: A range input for continuous values like volume or opacity.
body: A range input. Good for values that feel continuous, like volume or opacity. The thumb is small on purpose; put the current value next to the label so people don't have to squint at it. Volume setSlider(v[0])} aria-label="Volume"/> API Wraps @radix-ui/react-slider . Pass an array of two numbers if you want a min/max range with two thumbs. Default API

title: Toggle group
desc: A segmented control for mutually-exclusive options.
body: A segmented control. Use it when the options are mutually exclusive and you can fit them on one line. Past four options it gets cramped; a Select does that job better. Grid List Kanban Single toggle The standalone Toggle is for a single on/off action, like a bold button in a toolbar or a pin on a list item. B I With icons Toggle groups work well for toolbar-style controls. Pass icon elements as children for compact controls like alignment or view switchers. Default Single toggle With icons

title: Badge
desc: Small labels for status, counts, and category tags.
body: Small labels for status, counts, and category tags. Three variants, all monotone, because a badge that screams louder than the thing it's on defeats the point. Neutral Outline Solid Status labels Keep status text explicit. Monoset does not assign meaning to color, so add a dot only as a second visual cue. ))} Tags Dismissible tags for filters, categories, or multi-select inputs. × ))} In context Badges sit next to nav labels for unread counts, inside table cells for row status, or beside headings for version numbers. Variants Status labels Tags In context

title: Card
desc: Surface containers: outline, elevated, and inset variants.
body: A surface container. Outline by default. Use the elevated variant when a card needs to feel lifted off the page, or the inset variant inside forms and modals where the surrounding panel already does the grouping. Outline Elevated Inset

title: Avatar
desc: Initials on a neutral background. Three sizes, stackable.
body: Initials on a neutral background. Three sizes. Pass an image URL and it renders the photo; leave it blank and you get the initials fallback. They stack, and when the group gets crowded an overflow chip shows how many more. Three named sizes. sm for dense lists and table rows, md for cards and nav, lg for profiles and headers. ))} Image with initials fallback When src is set, the image renders. If it fails to load (broken URL, 404), the initials show instead. No layout shift either way. `} Stacked group Overlap avatars with a negative margin. The overflow chip at the end shows the remaining count. }/> ))} `} In a list A common pattern: avatar next to a name and subtitle in a compact row. Sizes Image with initials fallback Stacked group In a list

title: Table
desc: Table markup with button-based sortable headers and optional row selection.
body: Dense rows with hairline separators. Pass `maxHeight` and the wrapper caps its height with the header pinned. Narrower viewports scroll sideways. Sortable headers place the interaction on a real button and set aria-sort on the header cell. Your app owns the row ordering and passes the current direction back in. Pass maxHeight and the wrapper caps its height and scrolls. The thead stays pinned. Example Scrollable with sticky header

title: Alert & Toast
desc: Inline alerts and transient toasts, both monotone.
body: Inline alerts sit in the layout and stay there until the problem is fixed. Toasts pop in, say their piece, and disappear. Both stay monotone. No red-green traffic light; the icon and the copy do the work. Inline alert Toast Usage

title: Kbd
desc: Keyboard shortcut chips for menus and tooltips.
body: Renders a keyboard shortcut. The little ⌘K chip you see in search bars and menus. Use it anywhere a user might reach for the mouse when they didn't have to. The chips below light up when you press the matching key. ⌘K (or Ctrl+K ) opens the command palette. ))} : "Press something…"} setLastShortcut("Open documentation") }, , ]} /> In context Open command bar ⌘ K Sizes sm for inside body text, md (the default) for menus and tooltips. Try it: press a key In context Sizes

title: Kbd · Try it: press a key
Spinner
desc: A monotone loading indicator for waits over 400ms.
body: For waits longer than ~400ms when you don't have a better placeholder. Anything faster, skip it; a spinner that flashes for 80ms just looks broken. Pass a pixel value. Match the spinner to the text size around it. px ))} } /> Inside a button Put it in a button while the request is in flight. Leave the label in place; swapping "Save" for the spinner makes the button width jump and the eye catches it. `} Inline with text Works in any inline context. Match the size to the surrounding font. Sizes Inside a button Inline with text

title: Skeleton
desc: A placeholder shape while real content loads.
body: A placeholder shape while real content loads. Show one only if the wait is over 400ms; faster than that, just render the result. } /> from "@monoset/react"; `} Card skeleton Match the shape of the real content. Three lines, an avatar, a button: whatever the loaded state will show. Basic skeletons Card skeleton

title: Empty state
desc: A friendly message and CTA when there's nothing to show.
body: A friendly message when there's nothing to show. Title, one-line description, an action. That's the recipe. Don't apologize and don't write a paragraph. Basic empty state API

title: Progress
desc: A bar that fills as a measurable task makes progress.
body: A bar that fills as a task makes progress. Use it when you can measure how much work is left. If you can't, use Spinner. Basic progress API

title: Accordion
desc: Disclosure panels. Keyboard and screen reader behavior from Radix.
body: A disclosure pattern. The headings stay visible, the bodies collapse by default. Keyboard and screen reader behavior comes from Radix, so you don't have to wire any of it yourself. Is Monoset free? Yes. The tokens and components use the MIT license. Does it work with Tailwind? Yes. You can reference the CSS variables from utility classes. Can I theme it? Yes. Override semantic tokens at the root or inside a scope. API Props forward to @radix-ui/react-accordion . Set type="single" so only one panel opens at a time, or type="multiple" if you want people to open several. Default API

title: Collapsible
desc: A single open-or-closed disclosure for one optional region.
body: A single open-or-closed disclosure. One trigger, one panel. Reach for Accordion when you have a set of sections; reach for Collapsible when you have one optional region, like an advanced-settings block. Advanced settings Tune cache size, retry limits, and request timeouts. Most teams never touch these. The defaults are tuned for typical workloads. from "@monoset/react"; Advanced settings Tune cache size, retry limits, and request timeouts. `} API The root takes Radix Collapsible props: open , defaultOpen , onOpenChange , disabled . Example API

title: Carousel
desc: Horizontal slider with arrows and pagination dots.
body: Horizontal slider with arrow buttons and pagination dots. Each child becomes a slide; CSS scroll-snap keeps slides aligned. Optional autoplay for hero sections. ))} Dragging or trackpad scrolling updates the active slide after the track settles. Autoplay pauses while the carousel is hovered or focused, while the page is hidden, and when reduced motion is requested. Basic API

title: Dialog
desc: A modal that interrupts the user. Confirmations, simple forms, alerts.
body: A modal that interrupts the user. Use it for actions that need attention or confirmation. Built on Radix Dialog: focus trap, scroll lock, Escape to close. Open dialog The project and all its data will be permanently removed. Cancel Delete from "@monoset/react"; Open dialog The project and all its data will be permanently removed. Cancel Delete `} Dialog vs Sheet Use Dialog for short, focused actions: confirmations, simple forms, alerts. Use Sheet when the panel needs more room or feels like an extension of the page (filters, detail views, mobile menus). Basic dialog Dialog vs Sheet API

title: Sheet
desc: A slide-over panel from any edge. Built on Radix Dialog.
body: A slide-over panel that enters from any edge. It traps focus and locks page scrolling. Escape closes it and returns focus to the trigger. Use Sheet as the root, SheetTrigger to open it, and SheetContent for the panel. The side prop controls which edge it slides from. Open filters Apply from "@monoset/react"; Open filters Filter controls go here. Done `} All four sides Pass side="left" , "right" , "top" , or "bottom" . Left and right panels span the full height; top and bottom span the full width. The size prop sets the panel width (for left/right) or height (for top/bottom). Accepts a number (pixels) or any CSS length string. Basic sheet All four sides Custom size API Common patterns

title: Popover
desc: A floating panel anchored to a trigger. Inline forms, filter controls, color pickers.
body: A floating panel anchored to a trigger. Reach for it when content is too much for a Tooltip but not big enough to deserve its own Dialog: inline forms, filter controls, color pickers. Basic popover API on PopoverContent

title: Tooltip
desc: A short label shown on hover or focus. Two or three words, max.
body: A short label shown on hover or focus. Two or three words, max. If you need more, reach for HoverCard. from "@monoset/react"; // Wrap your app once ✓ `} The TooltipProvider belongs at the app root so all tooltips share the same delay timing. Monoset's MonosetProvider includes it for you. Basic tooltip API

title: HoverCard
desc: A rich tooltip shown on hover. For previewing what's behind a link or mention.
body: A rich tooltip. Use it when the preview content is too much for a one-line tooltip but not so much that it deserves its own page. Common cases: user mentions, link previews, ticket summaries. Hover over e.preventDefault()} style= }> @ada Ada Turing Designer · Joined 2026 Working on the new dashboard design. to see a card. from "@monoset/react"; @ada Ada Turing Designer · Joined 2026 `} Link preview Wrap any link to show a preview when the user hovers. Good for internal mentions, doc links, or external services where you want to show context before the click. Use Tooltip for a short label or keyboard hint. Use HoverCard for structured preview content. HoverCard opens from hover or focus, but the trigger should still make sense without the preview. The root HoverCard also accepts openDelay and closeDelay in milliseconds. Defaults are 700 and 300, the same as Radix. Basic usage Link preview HoverCard vs Tooltip API

title: Dropdown menu
desc: A vertical list of actions triggered by a button.
body: A vertical list of actions triggered by a button. For navigation use the sidebar instead. DropdownMenu is for actions on a row, a record, or the current view. Actions Account Profile ⌘P Billing Settings ⌘, Log out ⇧⌘Q from "@monoset/react"; Actions Account Profile Billing Log out `} DropdownMenu vs Popover If your panel is a list of clickable actions, use DropdownMenu . It has the right keyboard semantics (arrow keys, type-ahead, Enter to activate). For a panel containing inputs, sliders, or anything that isn't a menu of actions, use Popover . Use checkbox items for independent settings, radio items for one choice from a group, and submenus for a short second level. Basic menu DropdownMenu vs Popover Choices and submenus

title: Context menu
desc: Right-click menu with items, labels, and separators.
body: Right-click menu. Use it for row actions in tables, file lists, or any contextual operation that doesn't deserve a dedicated button. Built on Radix's ContextMenu primitive. Basic

title: Command palette
desc: A searchable command menu with keyboard navigation. Opens on Cmd+K.
body: A controlled, searchable command menu with announced keyboard navigation. Your app decides when to open it and what each action does. Pass an array of items, control the open state, and handle selection via onSelect on each item. setPaletteOpen(true)}>Open command palette from "@monoset/react"; import from "react"; function App() , , , , ]; return ( <> setOpen(true)}>Open commands ); }`} Grouped items Pass an array of groups instead of a flat list. Each group gets an optional heading. The palette itself does not register a global keyboard shortcut. Wire it up yourself so you control when it activates. By default the palette matches against label , description , and keywords . Pass a custom filter function to change this. Pass a footer prop to render keyboard hints or branding below the list. Basic usage Grouped items Keyboard shortcut Custom filter Footer API CommandItem shape

title: Tabs
desc: Underline tabs for switching between related panels.
body: Underline tabs for switching between related panels. Use them inside one view, not as a replacement for links between pages. Overview Activity Members Settings Overview content Activity content Members content Settings content Segmented choices Tabs ships one underline treatment. Use ToggleGroup for a compact segmented choice such as a time range. Day Week Month Year Controlled For controlled usage, pass value and onValueChange instead of defaultValue . Tabs wraps Radix UI Tabs. All Radix props are forwarded. Underline tabs Segmented choices Controlled

title: Navigation menu
desc: Header dropdown nav with rich content panes.
body: Header navigation with rich content panes. Radix handles its keyboard controls, focus, and ARIA roles. Basic

title: Breadcrumb
desc: Show where the current page sits in the hierarchy.
body: Show where the current page sits in the hierarchy. Two levels deep is fine, four is a smell. If you need that many, the navigation is too nested. from "@monoset/react"; `} The last item without an href is treated as the current page. Basic breadcrumb

title: Pagination
desc: Page-by-page navigation for tables and long lists.
body: Page-by-page navigation for tables and long lists. If you don't know the total upfront, prefer infinite scroll or load-more. Pagination needs a known total. Basic pagination API

title: Stepper
body: A horizontal multi-step indicator. Pass a list of steps and the current index — done steps get a checkmark, the current one gets the accent color, pending steps stay neutral. Basic API

title: Layout
desc: Stack, Inline, Grid, and Container primitives for spacing and structure.
body: Four layout primitives that handle spacing so you never write display: flex; gap: 16px by hand again. Stack for vertical, Inline for horizontal, Grid for auto-fit columns, Container for max-width centering. Vertical spacing. The gap prop maps to the --space-* token scale (0-14). ))} } /> Inline Horizontal with wrap. Good for tag lists, button rows, or any set of items that should flow left to right and wrap naturally. ))} `} Grid Auto-fit responsive grid. Cards wrap to the next row when they can't fit at minWidth . No breakpoints needed. ))} Container Centers content with a max-width. Five sizes from sm (640px) to 2xl (1536px). Adds horizontal padding by default. These primitives are designed to nest. A typical page layout stacks sections vertically, each section uses a Container, and the content inside uses Grid or Inline. Stack Inline Grid Container Composing layouts

title: AppShell
desc: A responsive application shell with one sidebar tree and a mobile drawer.
body: The shell every dashboard ends up needing: a sidebar, a sticky header, a scrollable content area, and a mobile drawer. Composed from sub-components so you can drop in your own brand and nav items. Five pieces compose the shell: AppShell as the root, AppShell.Sidebar as the left rail, and AppShell.Main wrapping the header and scrollable content. Below 768px the sidebar becomes a modal drawer and the hamburger trigger appears. The same sidebar DOM moves between both layouts, so forms and local state are not duplicated. Pass your router's current path as navigationSignal . A changed value closes an open mobile drawer and restores focus. Pass active on the current page's item. AppShell handles the visual style and sets aria-current="page" . You drive the routing. Pass any element as the icon prop. Lucide icons work well at 15-16px. Pass any element as footer . It sticks to the bottom of the sidebar with a separator above it. The default is 240px. Pass sidebarWidth on the root to change it. Use the hook when a control inside the shell needs to read or change the drawer directly. Prefer navigationSignal for route changes. Basic structure Responsive behavior Close after navigation Active state Icons Sidebar footer Custom sidebar width useAppShellMobile API

title: Separator
desc: A horizontal or vertical hairline. Use sparingly.
body: A horizontal or vertical hairline. Use it sparingly. Most layouts don't need them; spacing alone usually carries the weight. Reach for one when two regions need a clear boundary. Horizontal API

title: Aspect ratio
desc: Hold media at a fixed width-to-height ratio while it loads.
body: Holds media at a fixed width-to-height ratio so layouts don't jump while images load. Wrap an image, video, or map embed and pass the ratio you want. Example API

title: CLI
desc: Add Monoset components to your project as source files with the monoset CLI.
body: Copy one of the CLI's supported source groups into your project, then edit the files in place. Or use it without installing via npx : Run monoset init to scaffold the CSS entry point and get instructions for installing the token and component packages. This creates a src/monoset.css file that imports the token and component stylesheets. Then install the packages: Run monoset list to see the current registry. The CLI copies the selected source files and their registered local dependencies. Files land in src/components/monoset/ by default. The command prints any package dependencies you need to install. ", type:"flag", default:"src/components/monoset", desc:"Where to write the files." }, , , ]}/> How it works The CLI fetches registered source files from the Monoset repository's main branch. It skips existing files unless you pass --overwrite . The source-copy registry is a supported subset of @monoset/react . Inspect the diff whenever you overwrite local changes. Both approaches work. Use @monoset/react from npm if you want a standard dependency you can upgrade with a version bump. Use the CLI if you want to own the source and modify components in place. Install Initialize a project Add components Add every registered group List available components Options How it works CLI vs npm package

title: Playground
desc: Try Monoset components live. Adjust props, preview the result, copy the code.
body: Pick a component, adjust its props, and see the result. The generated code below is ready to paste into your project.

title: LLM naming prompt
desc: A prompt for adding stable data-ms attributes across a React codebase.
body: Paste the prompt below into any coding agent to add stable data-ms names to your app. With stable names in place, your Playwright tests, your analytics selectors, and your next design-tool integration stop breaking every time someone renames a className. Class names get renamed when someone reaches for a new CSS framework. Layouts get rewritten when a redesign lands. A data-ms named by what the thing is for , rather than how it looks, tends to survive both. Monoset uses this pattern internally: the landing page carries data-ms="bento-player" and data-ms="hero-title" , which is why the responsive CSS can target them without caring about the markup underneath. Run it on one page or feature at a time. Whole-app passes tend to produce generic names that all sound the same. Once they're in place, the rest is boring. Any tool that speaks CSS selectors can find what it needs: Class names are styling concerns. They change when you refactor CSS, swap to Tailwind, or rename a component. IDs are unique but brittle and overloaded (anchor links, ARIA, form controls). Data attributes are the only selector that carries no styling or behavioral meaning, which is exactly what makes them stable across rewrites. Three things benefit from stable selectors: Different UI patterns need different naming strategies. Here's what works for common Monoset patterns: A complete Playwright test that uses data-ms selectors. No fragile class names, no text matchers that break with copy changes. When your codebase has data-ms attributes, AI tools like Cursor, Copilot, and Claude Code can reference UI regions by name instead of guessing at the DOM structure. This makes prompts like "add a loading state to the users-table" unambiguous. Add a note to your project's AI instructions (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, etc.): Why semantic names The prompt Naming conventions Using the names Why design systems need stable selectors Component-specific conventions Playwright test example Using with AI coding tools

Theming Monoset
desc: Dark mode, accent colors, and scoped themes.
body: Monoset ships light and dark modes, scoped theme overrides, and a single accent channel you can swap in one line of CSS. Monoset flips the entire neutral ramp when the root element carries data-monoset-theme="dark" or the .monoset-dark class. Every semantic token (--bg, --fg1, --border, etc.) re-maps automatically. No extra imports, no separate stylesheet. Wrap your app in MonosetProvider and pass your default theme. The provider reads the user's system preference on first load and syncs it to localStorage. Read and set the current theme from anywhere inside the provider tree. Drop in the built-in toggle for a sun/moon switch that works out of the box. You can force dark mode on a single container without touching the rest of the page. Add the .monoset-dark class to any element, and every Monoset token inside it flips. Monoset uses three CSS custom properties for the accent color channel: --accent , --accent-fg , and --accent-hover . Override them at any scope to swap the brand color without rebuilding anything. Every Monoset component that uses accent (primary buttons, focused inputs, active tabs) picks up the new values automatically. How dark mode works Scoped dark mode Customizing the accent

Accessibility checklist
desc: What Monoset handles, what you handle, and how to test.
body: Monoset supplies component-level behavior, not a blanket guarantee for the finished page. This checklist separates what the package implements from what your application must provide and verify. Use vitest-axe to catch regressions in your own pages. The test below checks that a rendered settings page has no accessibility violations. Test with a keyboard and a screen reader before shipping. Page content and application composition are still your responsibility: What Monoset handles Testing with axe What you need to add

title: Accessibility checklist · What you need to add
MCP server
desc: Give your AI coding agent direct access to Monoset components, tokens, and docs.
body: Make Monoset's bundled component catalog, tokens, and docs index available to an MCP client. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard way for AI tools to talk to external systems. Monoset's MCP server exposes five tools your agent can call: Claude Code picks up MCP servers from your project settings or global config. Add Monoset to your project: Claude Code can now call the five lookup tools in this guide. Add this to your Claude Desktop config file: Restart Claude Desktop after saving the config. Add a .cursor/mcp.json file with monoset-mcp as the stdio command. Ask for the data the server actually exposes: The server returns reference data. Your agent still needs to read the component types and verify the code it writes. The server gives an MCP client a searchable index of the package instead of making it infer component names or token values. The MCP catalog helps an agent choose package exports. data-ms attributes from the LLM naming guide give it stable selectors in your application. What this does Install Claude Code Claude Desktop Cursor Useful queries Why this matters Combine with LLM naming

Using with Next.js
desc: App Router, Pages Router, and Server Component setup.
body: Set up Monoset with the App Router or Pages Router. A short Remix example follows. Keep the root layout as a Server Component. Import the styles there, then pass the page through a small client provider. ); }`} from "@monoset/react"; import type from "react"; export function Providers( : ) ); }`} Pages Router setup For the Pages Router, put the same styles and provider in _app.tsx . Pages Router does not use the App Router's "use client" directive. Render components that use hooks, browser APIs, or event handlers from a client component. Keep the boundary around the interactive part of the page. Layout primitives like Container , Stack , and Card are plain HTML wrappers with no interactivity. You can render them in Server Components without a client boundary. Remix uses a similar pattern. Import the CSS in your root route and wrap the Outlet with the provider. Building a settings page
desc: A real-world pattern composing Tabs, Cards, and form controls.
body: A settings page is a good stress test for any component library. It needs tabs, form controls, cards, validation, and a layout that holds up on mobile. Here is how the pieces fit together in Monoset. Tabs across the top, cards for each section, and a save bar pinned to the bottom. This is the pattern most teams end up with. Field handles accessible labels and errors. Keep form state in React or use the form library your app already has. Container caps the width and centers the content. Stack handles vertical spacing between form fields. Both are thin wrappers that apply Monoset spacing tokens so you don't end up with magic numbers in every file. Full settings layout Form validation Layout with Stack and Container

Building a dashboard
desc: Sidebar, stat cards, and a data table in one layout.
body: A sidebar, a header, stat cards, and a data table. This is the layout you end up building for every SaaS admin panel. Here's how it comes together with Monoset. The outer frame is a CSS grid: a fixed sidebar on the left, the main content area on the right. The header sits inside the content area, not spanning the full width. A vertical nav with grouped links. The active item gets a muted background. A responsive grid of summary metrics. The Grid component handles the responsive layout; each card is just a Card with a title, value, and optional trend indicator. Below the stat cards, a sortable table with row selection. Use TableHeader for sortable columns and TableSelectAll / TableSelectRow for the checkboxes. The full dashboard page composes all three sections inside the shell. The shell Sidebar Stat cards Data table Putting it together

Data table with filters
desc: Searchable, sortable, filterable table with row selection and bulk actions.
body: A searchable, filterable, sortable data table with row selection and bulk actions. The pattern you need every time someone says "can we add an admin view for this?" A table of users with: a search input that filters by name or email, a select dropdown that filters by role, sortable column headers, row checkboxes, and a bulk action bar that appears when rows are selected. An Input above the table. Filter rows on each keystroke. A Select for role filtering. Combine it with the search filter. Use TableHeader with an onSort callback. Keep the sort state in a single object. Track selected row IDs in a Set. Show the bulk action bar only when the set is non-empty. Everything wired together. What we're building Search filter Column filter Sort Row selection and bulk actions Full example
